Lab 1: The Apollo Missions¶

Introduction¶

Imagine you are an engineer working on the very early days of the Apollo program. In order to send people to the moon, you need a basic understanding of the gravitational potential and forces the mission will experience. You also need to start quantifying the performance of the new rocket that will carry the capsule: the Saturn V.

Your team at NASA has been tasked with conducting a series of investigations, which you are guided through below. You will make some plots, and also write some short paragraphs with your scientific conclusions. The NASA Director Gene Kranz will use your notebook to defend NASA funding to congress. So your answers should be short and to the point, quantitative in nature, and contain highly polished plots, with appropriate titles, units, etc.

Other important requirements:

  • Your final notebook---BOTH the .ipynb file AND the html export---should be COMMITTED and PUSHED to your fork of the PHYS265-spring26 GitHub repository. Instructions for doing this are available online.
  • All plotting should be done in object oriented fashion, as discussed in class.

Any use of “plt.plot()” will result in zero points being awarded for that question.

  • Before submitting your work, reset JLD and then re-run your final code (Kernel → Restart Kernel and Run All Cells…) Make sure your code executes as intended and without error. The first cell should be "1", and the cells should execute in order.

A table of relevant physical constants is below. Good luck!

Quantity Value
Gravitational Constant ($G$) $6.67 \times 10^{-11}$ m³/kg/s²
Gravitational Acceleration ($g$) 9.81 m/s²
Mass of the Earth ($M_E$) $5.9 \times 10^{24}$ kg
Mass of the Moon ($M_M$) $7.3 \times 10^{22}$ kg
Mass of Apollo Command Module 5500 kg
Radius of the Earth ($R_E$) 6378 km
Radius of the Moon ($R_M$) 1737 km
Distance from Earth to Moon ($d_{EM}$) $3.8 \times 10^{8}$ m
Exhaust Velocity of Saturn V Stage 1 (S-1C) ($v_e$) $2.4 \times 10^{3}$ m/s
Burn Rate of S-1C ($\dot{m}$) $1.3 \times 10^{4}$ kg/s
Wet Mass of S-1C ($m_0$) $2.8 \times 10^{6}$ kg
Dry Mass of S-1C ($m_f$) $7.5 \times 10^5$ kg
In [1]:
#show
# In the code cell beneath this one you can put your imports,
# and define any variables you might need (e.g. the mass of the earth)
In [221]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import scipy as scipy

# All variables are in SI units
G = 6.67e-11 
g = 9.81
M_earth = 5.9e24
M_moon = 7.3e22
M_apollo = 5500
R_earth = 6.378e6
R_moon = 1.737e6
dem = 3.8e8
v_exhaust = 2.4e3
rate_burn = 1.3e4
mass_wet = 2.8e6
mass_dry = 7.5e5

Part 1: The Gravitational Potential of the Earth¶

The gravitational potential at a distance $r$ from a mass $M$ is given by:

$$\Phi(r) = -\frac{GM}{r}$$

where G is the gravitational constant.

  1. Code up a function that takes as an input the mass of a body $M$ , the cartesian location of the

mass $(x_M , y_M)$, and the location of some distant evaluation point $(x, y)$. Have the function return the potential at the evaluation point. You may consider approximating the Earth as a point particle, but in which case, be careful! For in that approximation, the the potential contains a singularity. In our function, use all modern best practices for coding. E.g. type hints, type checks, pre and post conditions, doc strings, etc.

In [223]:
# Defining a function
def pot(M,xM,yM,x,y):
    """ A function that returns the gravitational potential at a distance r from a celestial body with mass M
    Args: 
        M: the mass of the celestial body
        xM: the x coordinate of the celestial body
        yM: the y coordinate of the celestial body
        x: the x coordinate of the distant point
        y: the y coordinate of the distant point
    Returns:
        the gravitational potential
    """
    r = np.sqrt((x-xM)**2 +(y-yM)**2)
    if np.any(np.isclose(r,0)):
        raise TypeError('r cannot be 0')

    potential = -1*G*M/r
    return potential
    
#print(f"The gravitational potential at point ({x},{y}) is {potential:.e} J/kg")
  1. Make a 1D plot of the absolute value of the gravitational potential $|\Phi|$ as a function of distance $x$ from the surface of the Earth, assuming $y = 0$.

Plot out to $1.5\times$ the distance from the Earth to the Moon. Because the potential falls quickly with r, make the y-axis logarithmic to visualize it better.

In [225]:
# Creating Arrays for Plotting:
x = np.linspace(R_earth,1.5*dem,1000)
y = np.zeros(1000)
pot_E = pot(M_earth,0,0,x,y)

# Plotting:
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.grid(True, alpha = 0.3)
ax.set(xlabel = "Distance (m)", ylabel = "Potential (J/kg)", title = "Gravitational Potential as a Function of the Distance from the Surface of the Earth")
ax.set_yscale("symlog")
ax.plot(x, pot_E, linestyle = "", marker = "x", color = "darkseagreen", markersize = 4, label = "Potential vs. Distance")
ax.legend(loc = "upper left")
plt.show()
  1. Then, make a 2D color-mesh plot of the potential $\Phi$ with the Earth at the origin. Plot $|\Phi|$

over a large range of $x$ and $y$, for example, from $-1.5d_{EM}$ to $+1.5d_{EM}$ . Force the plot to have a square aspect ratio. Make sure to add a nice colorbar, and make the colorbar logarithmic.

In [227]:
# Creating New Arrays:
x1 = np.concatenate([np.linspace(-1.5*dem,-1,500),np.linspace(1,1.5*dem,500)])  # Combining two arrays together to avoid the position (0,0)
y1 = np.concatenate([np.linspace(-1.5*dem,-1,500),np.linspace(1,1.5*dem,500)])

X, Y = np.meshgrid(x1,y1)
Z = pot(M_earth,0,0,X,Y)
Z_log = np.log10(-Z)  # A lot of time went in to making more of the color spectrum appear on the graph, as many times it was all yellow or all purple!

vmin = np.percentile(Z_log, 1)  # Gives a nicer spread of color. 
vmax = np.percentile(Z_log, 99)   

# Plotting
from matplotlib.colors import SymLogNorm
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
mesh = ax.pcolormesh(X,Y,Z_log, shading = "auto", cmap = "plasma", vmin=vmin, vmax=vmax)
fig.colorbar(mesh, ax=ax, label = "log10(|Gravitational Potential|)")
ax.set(xlabel = "x-position (m)", ylabel = "y-position", title = "Gravitational Potential around the Earth")
ax.plot(0,0, "kx", markersize = 10, label = "Earth Center of Mass")
ax.legend()
plt.show()

Part 2: The Gravitational Potential of the Earth-Moon System¶

Now, imagine the Earth is at the origin $(x_{E},y_{E}) = (0,0)$, and the moon is at a location $(x_{M},y_{M}) = \big( \frac{d_{EM}}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{d_{EM}}{\sqrt{2}} \big)$.

  1. Make an updated 2D color-mesh plot, from $-1.5d_{EM}$ to $+1.5d_{EM}$,

of the gravitational potential of the combined Earth-Moon system.

In [229]:
x2 = np.concatenate([np.linspace(-1.5*dem,-1,500),np.linspace(1,1.5*dem,500)])  # Combining two arrays together to avoid the position (0,0)
y2 = np.concatenate([np.linspace(-1.5*dem,-1,500),np.linspace(1,1.5*dem,500)])

X2, Y2 = np.meshgrid(x2,y2)
Z2 = pot(M_earth,0,0,X2,Y2) + pot(M_moon, dem/np.sqrt(2), dem/np.sqrt(2), X2, Y2)
Z2_log = np.log10(-Z2)  

vmin = np.percentile(Z2_log, 1)  # So that more colors show up.
vmax = np.percentile(Z2_log, 99)   

# Plotting
from matplotlib.colors import SymLogNorm
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
mesh = ax.pcolormesh(X2,Y2,Z2_log, shading = "auto", cmap = "plasma", vmin=vmin, vmax=vmax)
fig.colorbar(mesh, ax=ax, label = "log10(|Gravitational Potential|)")
ax.set(xlabel = "x-position (m)", ylabel = "y-position", title = "Gravitational Potential around the Earth")
ax.plot(0,0, "kx", markersize = 10, label = "Earth Center of Mass")
ax.plot(dem/np.sqrt(2), dem/np.sqrt(2), "kx", markersize = 3, label = "Moon Center of Mass")
ax.legend()
plt.show()
  1. Additionally, make a 2D contour plot over the same $x$ and $y$ range.

Do not let all the contours bunch up around the Earth and Moon. You will probably again need to tell matplotlib to use a logarithmic color scale.

In [233]:
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
levels = np.percentile(Z2_log, np.linspace(1, 99, 50)) # So that more colors show up
contour = ax.contourf(X, Y, Z2_log, levels=levels, cmap="plasma", extend='both')
fig.colorbar(contour, ax=ax, label="log10(|Potential|)")
ax.plot(0, 0, 'kx', markersize=8, label = "Location of the Earth")
ax.plot(dem/np.sqrt(2), dem/np.sqrt(2), 'kx', markersize=3, label = "Location of the Moon")
ax.set_aspect('equal')
ax.legend(loc = 'upper left')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Part 3: The Gravitational Force Field of the Earth-Moon System¶

The gravitational force $\vec{F}$ that a mass $M_1$ exerts on a mass $m_2$ is:

$$\vec{F}_{21} = -G \frac{M_1 m_2}{|\vec{r}_{21}|^2} \hat{r}_{21}$$

where $\vec{r}_{21}$ is the displacement vector from $M_1$ to $m_2$.

  1. Code up a function that takes as an input the mass of a body $M_1$,

the mass of a secondary body $m_2$, the cartesian location of the first mass $(x_1, y_1)$, and the cartesian location of the second body $(x_2, y_2)$. Have the function return the two gravitational force components, $F_x$ and $F_y$, experienced by the mass $m_2$ from the force exerted by $M_1$. Note that the gravitational force contains a singularity, and so if you made the approximation that the Earth is a finite point, you will need to avoid it. Like for the 1D potential, make sure it follows modern best-coding practices.

In [251]:
def grav(M,m,xM,yM,xm,ym):
    """ A function that gives the force of gravity between two masses.
    Args: 
        M: the mass of the larger mass
        m: the mass of the smaller mass
        xM: the x-coordinate of the larger mass
        yM: the y-coordinate of the larger mass
        xm: the x-coordinate of the smaller mass
        ym: the y-coordinate of the smaller mass
    Returns:
        Returns gravitational force vector components between two masses.
    """
    r = np.sqrt((xm-xM)**2 +(ym-yM)**2)
    rx = xm - xM
    ry = ym - yM
    
    if np.any(np.isclose(r,0)):
        raise TypeError('r cannot be 0')
        
    Fg = -G*M*m/(r**2)   
    Fx = Fg*rx/r
    Fy = Fg*ry/r
    
    return Fx, Fy
  1. Assuming the Earth is still at the origin and the Moon is still at

$(x_M, y_M) = \big( \frac{d_{EM}}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{d_{EM}}{\sqrt{2}} \big)$, make a 2D streamplot of the gravitational force the Earth-Moon system will exert on the Apollo 11 command module. Plot the force over the same x-y range of $-1.5 d_{EM}$ to $+1.5 d_{EM}$. Add a nice color bar, which will again probably need to be logarithmic. Mark the location of the earth with a black circle, and the moon with a black square. Add a legend.

In [279]:
# Making the arrays:
x3 = np.linspace(-1.5*dem,1.5*dem,250)  # Combining two arrays together to avoid the position (0,0)
y3 = np.linspace(-1.5*dem,1.5*dem,250)
X3, Y3 = np.meshgrid(x3,y3)

# Force Components:
Fx_earth, Fy_earth = grav(M_earth, M_apollo, 0, 0, X3, Y3)
Fx_moon, Fy_moon = grav(M_moon, M_apollo, dem/np.sqrt(2), dem/np.sqrt(2), X3, Y3)

# Combining for total force:
Fx = Fx_earth + Fx_moon
Fy = Fy_earth + Fy_moon

# For coloring:
F_mag = np.sqrt((Fx**2)+(Fy**2))
F_mag_log = np.log10(F_mag)

# For better color spread:
vmin = np.percentile(F_mag_log, 0.1)
vmax = np.percentile(F_mag_log, 99.9)

# Plotting:
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
stream = ax.streamplot(X3, Y3, Fx, Fy, color = F_mag_log, cmap = "plasma", norm=plt.Normalize(vmin=vmin, vmax=vmax), density = 2)
fig.colorbar(stream.lines, ax=ax, label="log10(|Force|) (N)")
ax.plot(0,0, 'ko', markersize = 8, label = "Earth")
ax.plot(dem/np.sqrt(2), dem/np.sqrt(2), 'ks', markersize = 4, label = "Moon")
ax.legend(loc = "upper left")
plt.show()
  1. You will note there is a point between the Earth and the Moon where the field lines seem to vanish. This is the point where the Earth's and Moon's gravitational forces cancel eachother. By setting $|\vec{F}_{moon}| = |\vec{F}_{earth}|$, calculate the radial distance of the "balance point." From there, identify the $x$ and $y$ coordinates of the balance point. Typeset your derivation (show multiple steps!) in latex in a markdown cell below. Then, calculate it numerically, and draw the answer onto a new version of your force field plot. Again mark the location of the earth with a black circle, and the moon with a black square; mark the location of the balance point with a red x. Add a legend.

Your Derivation Goes in the markdown cell below:

To find the radial distance of the "balance point":

  1. First set the forces equal to each other, where $G$ is the universal gravitational constant, $M_{E}$ is the mass of the Earth, $m$ is the mass of some object, $D_{E}$ is the distance to the Earth, and $D_{M}$ is the distance to the moon:
\begin{equation} \frac{-G M_{E} m}{D_{E}^{2}} = \frac{-G M_{M} m}{D_{M}^{2}} \tag{1} \end{equation}
  1. Cancel out like terms:
\begin{equation} \frac{M_{E}}{D_{E}^{2}} = \frac{M_{M}}{D_{M}^{2}} \tag{2} \end{equation}
  1. Find $D_{M}$ in terms of $D_{E}$.
\begin{equation} D_{M} = D_{E}\sqrt \frac{M_{M}}{M_{E}} \tag{3} \end{equation}
  1. Then bring in a second equation, where $d_{EM}$ is the distance between the Earth and the Moon:
\begin{equation} D_{M} +D_{E} = d_{EM} \tag{4} \end{equation}
  1. Substitute and Solve:
\begin{equation} D_{E} = \frac{d_{EM}}{\sqrt \frac{M_{M}}{M_{E}}+1} \tag{5} \end{equation}

From equations (5) and (3) you can plug in exact values and solve.

And your updated plot, including the balance point, goes in the cell below.

In [307]:
de = dem/((np.sqrt(M_moon/M_earth))+1)
dm = de*(np.sqrt(M_moon/M_earth))

print(f"The balance point is {de:e} m from the Earth and {dm:e} m from the Moon")

# Updated Plot:
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
stream = ax.streamplot(X3, Y3, Fx, Fy, color = F_mag_log, cmap = "plasma", norm=plt.Normalize(vmin=vmin, vmax=vmax), density = 2)
fig.colorbar(stream.lines, ax=ax, label="log10(|Force|) (N)")
ax.plot(0,0, 'ko', markersize = 10, label = "Earth")
ax.plot(dem/np.sqrt(2), dem/np.sqrt(2), 'ks', markersize = 6, label = "Moon")
ax.plot(de/np.sqrt(2), de/np.sqrt(2), "x", color = "lime", markersize = 10, label = "Balance Point") # Changed the x to green to make it easier to see
ax.legend(loc = "upper left")
plt.show()

The balance point is 3.419623e+08 m from the Earth and 3.803765e+07 m from the Moon
  1. Quantitatively discuss your conclusions. Is the balance point close to the Moon or Earth? How close? Does this make sense?

Your Answer Goes in the markdown cell below:

The balance point is close to the moon, only $3.8 \times 10^{7} m$ away from the moon compared to $3.4 \times 10^{8} m$ away from the earth. This makes sense because the Earth is almost 100 times more massive than the Moon. The r is squared in the denominator, which suggests that the point should be roughly 10 times closer to the Moon than to the Earth, and that is about what we get.

Part 4: Altitude of the Saturn V Rocket¶

Rockets function by conservation of momentum. The ejection of fuel backwards propels the rocket forwards. The change in a rocket's velocity $\Delta v$ as a function of time $t$ (under a set of simplifying assumptions) is given by the Tsiolkovsky rocket equation:

$$\Delta v(t) = v_e \ln \left(\frac{m_0}{m(t)}\right) - gt$$

where $m_0$ is the initial "wet" mass (fuel + rocket parts + payload), $m(t) = m_0 - \dot{m}t$ is the mass at time $t$, $\dot{m}$ is the fuel burn rate (which we will assume to be constant), $v_e$ is the fuel exhaust velocity, and $g$ is the gravitational acceleration.

You can find the altitude $h$ of the rocket at "burnout" (when all the fuel is used up) by integrating the velocity over time:

$$h = \int_{0}^{T} \Delta v(t) \, dt$$

where $T$ is the total burn time of the rocket, which by conservation of momentum arguments is:

$$T = \frac{m_0 - m_f}{\dot{m}}$$

where $m_f$ is the final "dry mass" of the rocket, once all the fuel is burned.

In [310]:
# Copying the variables since they are far away
G = 6.67e-11 
g = 9.81
M_earth = 5.9e24
M_moon = 7.3e22
M_apollo = 5500
R_earth = 6.378e6
R_moon = 1.737e6
dem = 3.8e8
v_exhaust = 2.4e3
rate_burn = 1.3e4
mass_wet = 2.8e6
mass_dry = 7.5e5
  1. Calculate the burn time $T$ for the first stage of

the new Saturn V rocket that NASA is planning to use to carry the Apollo capsule.

In [346]:
T = (mass_wet - mass_dry)/rate_burn

print(f"The total burn time for the Saturn V rocket is {T:.2f} s")
print(mass_wet-(rate_burn*T))

The total burn time for the Saturn V rocket is 157.69 s
750000.0000000002
  1. Define a function that returns the change in velocity $\Delta v(t)$.

It should take all necessary arguments, meaning: the time, wet mass, dry mass, burn rate, exhaust speed, and gravitational constant $g$. Have it return the change in velocity. Note that the rocket eventually runs out of fuel, so you should design your function carefully so that $\Delta v$ eventually becomes zero after all fuel is spent. Again, document and guard your function according to best practices.

In [352]:
def delvel(t, ve, m0, mdot):               # Took a really long time before I realized t had to be the first variable :(
    """ A function that gives the change in velocity of a rocket.
    Args: 
        ve: the fuel exhaust velocity
        m0: the wet mass
        mdot: the fuel burn rate
        t: the time
    Returns:
        The change in velocity of a rocket.
    """
    deltav = ve*np.log(m0/(m0-(mdot*t)))-(g*t)
    return deltav
  1. Compute the altitude of the rocket at the end of the burn.

Do this by numerically integrating from $t=0$ (launch) to $t=T$ (burnout). Use the scipy.integrate.quad() function.

In [354]:
h, _ = scipy.integrate.quad(delvel, 0, T, args = (v_exhaust, mass_wet, rate_burn))

print(f"The altitude that the rocket will reach is {h} m!")

The altitude that the rocket will reach is 74093.98013366401 m!
  1. Just last week, NASA got the test results back from the first prototype

of Saturn V. They found that it burned for about 160 seconds and lifted the system to an altitude of about 70km. Director Kranz has asked you to compare your calculations for $T$ and $h$ to the testing data. You should offer some explanations about why your calculation is an over or under estimate, e.g. neglecting drag.

Your Answer Goes in the markdown cell below:

My answer for the altitude was an overestimate. Drag from the atmosphere is probably the main cause of this discrepancy, as it is another downwards force on the rocket, preventing more energy from being converted into gravitational potential energy. Another reason why I may have overestimated is due to the fact that the rocket may not be going straight up the whole time, it may be going at an angle, so there will be some x and y displacement instead of just z. My answer for the burnout time was an underestimate. This is probably because it assumes an ideal fuel consumption scenario, while a true rocket will be less than ideal.

In [ ]:
